description:
You are a newly-hired backup engineer.  
    Management has asked you to create a *reproducible* compressed backup of the
    directory

        /home/user/important_data

    and to record verifiable proof of its integrity.  
    The steps below describe exactly what the company’s automation will look
    for.  Follow them in order.

    1.  Create a directory called /home/user/backups if it does **not** already
        exist.  All artefacts produced by this task must live in this directory.

    2.  Produce a **gzip-compressed TAR archive** of /home/user/important_data
        named exactly

            /home/user/backups/important_data_backup.tar.gz

        The company’s policies require this archive to be *byte-for-byte
        reproducible*, so the following `tar` options **must** be respected
        (do not omit any of them):

            --sort=name          (files sorted alphabetically)
            --owner=0            (set uid to 0 in the tar header)
            --group=0            (set gid to 0 in the tar header)
            --numeric-owner      (store numeric ids, not names)
            --mtime="UTC 2020-01-01"   (fixed timestamp for every entry)

        If you have to invoke gzip manually, use the default compression level
        (no extra flags).

    3.  Generate a SHA-256 checksum of the archive with the canonical
        two-column format produced by the command

            sha256sum important_data_backup.tar.gz

        Save that single line of output into the file

            /home/user/backups/important_data_backup.sha256

        The line **must** contain two consecutive spaces between the checksum
        and the filename, e.g.

            <64-hex-digits><two spaces>important_data_backup.tar.gz

    4.  Immediately verify the checksum using

            sha256sum -c important_data_backup.sha256

        (or an equivalent method).  Capture the verification result in a small
        human-readable log.

    5.  Create /home/user/backups/backup_log.txt with **exactly four lines**,
        no extra blank lines.  The required format is:

        • Line 1 – an RFC-3339 / ISO-8601 timestamp in UTC, e.g.
                     2024-01-30T14:22:05Z  
        • Line 2 – the absolute path of the archive, i.e.
/home/user/backups/important_data_backup.tar.gz
        • Line 3 – the 64-character SHA-256 checksum in hex  
        • Line 4 – the literal text “Verification: OK” (if the checksum test
          passed) or “Verification: FAILED” (if it did not)

        Example:

            2024-01-30T14:22:05Z
            /home/user/backups/important_data_backup.tar.gz
            d2c49d9…(60 more hex chars)…
            Verification: OK

    6.  Do **not** delete or rename any file inside /home/user/important_data.

    Success criteria that the automated grader will check:

        a) /home/user/backups/important_data_backup.tar.gz exists, is a valid
           gzipped tar archive, and unpacks to the exact directory structure
           found in /home/user/important_data.
        b) /home/user/backups/important_data_backup.sha256 exists, contains
           exactly one line in the required two-column format, and the checksum
           recorded inside matches the archive’s actual SHA-256 digest.
        c) /home/user/backups/backup_log.txt exists and follows precisely the
           four-line format described above.
        d) The checksum quoted in the log’s third line is *identical* to the
           checksum inside important_data_backup.sha256.
        e) The fourth line of the log is literally “Verification: OK”.

    Remember: create the archive with the deterministic options, write the
    checksum file, verify it, and then populate the four-line log.
truth:
<!--
        Ground-truth set-up and expected properties
        ===========================================

        1. Initial directory tree (already present when the agent starts):

            /home/user/important_data
            ├── config
            │   └── settings.conf
            ├── projectA
            │   ├── data.csv
            │   └── report.txt
            └── projectB
                ├── figures
                │   └── diagram.png
                └── presentation.pptx

        2. Exact file contents (small text files only):
           • /home/user/important_data/config/settings.conf
                 ---
                 # Application settings
                 mode = production
                 timeout = 30
                 ---
           • /home/user/important_data/projectA/report.txt
                 Project A quarterly report — draft
           • /home/user/important_data/projectA/data.csv
                 id,value
                 1,42
                 2,97
           (binary files projectB/presentation.pptx and
            projectB/figures/diagram.png can be any non-empty content;
            the grader does not open them, only checks for their presence.)

        3. Directory /home/user/backups does NOT exist before the task begins.

        4. After the agent finishes successfully:

           a) /home/user/backups/important_data_backup.tar.gz
              • is produced with the required deterministic tar options.
              • When extracted, it reproduces the tree in §1 verbatim.

           b) /home/user/backups/important_data_backup.sha256
              • contains one line:
                    <sha256sum><two spaces>important_data_backup.tar.gz

           c) /home/user/backups/backup_log.txt
              • has 4 non-empty lines.
              • Line 2 equals “/home/user/backups/important_data_backup.tar.gz”.
              • Line 3 equals the checksum recorded in the .sha256 file.
              • Line 4 is literally “Verification: OK”.

        5. Permissions: all new files should be readable by the current user;
           the grader runs as the same user and therefore has access.

        6. The grader will recompute the checksum of
           important_data_backup.tar.gz and compare it to:
               – the checksum in important_data_backup.sha256
               – the checksum on line 3 of backup_log.txt
           and will confirm that they match.
    -->
